what_it_does: An interpretive ledger entry for an archived v1 presentation stylesheet; it records that the sheet composes zone tokens into a tonal-zoning deliverable page, including a masthead, a zone-registry card catalog, a shared button/chip/Vi-pill vocabulary, and a full fixed-width Overview-dashboard mock, and it collects observations, conventions, concerns, themes, and inferred intent for that CSS file.
observations: The ledger states the sheet consumes zone tokens from colors_and_type.css and never invents color.
It documents a radial-gradient body ground, a masked 64px architectural grid, and pointer-events-none spatial/ghost/flow decorative layers.
Component states (.cvx-btn, .cvx-chip, .ov-block) are mapped to named zone role tokens rather than ad-hoc colors.
The Overview mock is a fixed 1240px-wide, three-column layout (nav / main / gold Vi rail) built to scale from transform-origin top-left.
Gold tokens are used consistently for the Vi rail, active nav item, stat values, and diamond gem motif.
A local --zone-ink / --zone-edge / --zone-surface override pattern lets generic cards inherit any zone accent.
The file is marked as archived (_archive-v1), indicating it is superseded.
standouts: Records a single sheet that renders both a token-catalog view and a fully applied dashboard mock.
Notes ghosted off-frame continuation panels and connective path layers that imply a spatial/branching workspace metaphor.
Highlights a local zone-variable override pattern for theming generic cards.
conventions: Compose-only: consumes tokens, never defines color.
Token-driven: values are referenced via var(--…).
Zone vocabulary reuse: component states map to named zone roles.
Decorative spatial layers are pointer-events:none and gated by a single --spatial-opacity knob.
concerns: The described CSS contains hardcoded geometry, including a fixed 1240px width and 760px min-height for the mock.
Some literal rgba()/percentage values in box-shadow composites sit outside the token system.
The sheet is archived, so a maintained consumer would need a v2 equivalent.
A large share of the rules are mock-specific (.ov-*) and tightly coupled to one demo dashboard layout.
notes: An archived interpretive record of the v1 tonal-zoning presentation sheet, documenting how it composes zone tokens into a catalog-plus-dashboard proof surface.
questions: Is the 1240px Overview mock a one-off demo or a template that later became real components?
What do the spatial/ghost/flow path layers represent semantically, and where would their data come from?
Why was this archived — did the zoning presentation move into the React component layer in v2?
purpose_vs_actual: Matches: the ledger states the sheet is a deliverable-presentation layer that composes zone roles, and the recorded observations describe exactly that.
intention_signals: Inferred: this ledger captures a showcase/proof surface meant to validate the tonal-zoning language by applying its tokens end-to-end in a realistic product screen with nav, zoned content, a source feed, and a gold Vi recommendation rail.
novelty: The recorded sheet combines a token-catalog view and a fully applied dashboard mock in one composition-only CSS file, plus ghosted off-frame continuation and connective path layers that express a spatial workspace.
contribution: Records the applied vocabulary layer — how zone tokens render as catalog cards, how shared components map to zone roles, and how a concrete dashboard composition (nav / content zones / source feed / gold Vi rail) coheres with grid-ground and spatial-continuation motifs.
summary_for_embedding: Interpretive ledger for an archived v1 tonal-zoning CSS file that documents a token-driven presentation sheet composing zone roles into a masthead, zone-registry catalog, component vocabulary, and a fixed 1240px Overview-dashboard mock with masked grid ground and ghosted spatial continuation layers.
intention_for_embedding: Capture the v1 proof-of-concept surface that demonstrates the tonal-zoning token model by rendering it as both a catalog and a realistic product dashboard.
